text: This Order may be called the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984. It extends to the whole of Pakistan and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any court, including a court-martial, but not to proceedings before an arbitrator.
In this Order, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context, various terms are defined including 'court', 'conclusive proof', 'document', 'evidence', and others.

A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the court believes it to exist or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought to act upon the supposition that it exists.

Evidence includes all statements which the court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry, and all documents produced for inspection of the court.
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other facts as are declared to be relevant.
Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction are relevant.
Facts which are the occasion, cause, or effect of facts in issue or relevant facts, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, are relevant.
Facts which show or constitute motive, preparation, or conduct of any party are relevant.
Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested, or which establish the identity of anything or person, are relevant.

Where there is reasonable ground to believe that two or more persons have conspired together to commit an offence or an actionable wrong, anything said, done, or written by any one of such persons is a relevant fact.

Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant if they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact or make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.
In suits in which damages are claimed, any fact which will enable the court to determine the amount of damages to be awarded is relevant.
Where the question is as to the existence of any right or custom, the following facts are relevant: transactions in which the right or custom was asserted, claimed, or denied.
Facts showing the existence of any state of mind such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, etc., when such state of mind is in issue or relevant.
Facts bearing on the question whether an act was accidental or intentional are relevant.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is relevant.
Admissions are statements, oral or documentary, which suggest any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, made by any of the persons and under the circumstances mentioned in this Article.
Statements made by a party to the proceeding, his agent, or by persons with proprietary interest in the subject-matter are admissions.
Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove against a party, and which are relevant to such position or liability, are admissions.
Statements made by persons expressly referred to by a party to a suit, as related to any fact in issue, are admissions.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but may operate as estoppel under the provisions hereinafter contained.

No admission is relevant in a civil case, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given or under circumstances from which the court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.
An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under Article 46.
Confession caused by inducement, threat or promise is irrelevant in criminal proceedings.
No confession made to a police officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst in the custody of a police officer is admissible unless made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate.

When any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, so much of such information as relates distinctly to the fact discovered may be proved.

If such a confession as is referred to in Articles 24, 25, and 26 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the court, been fully removed, it is relevant.
Confession otherwise relevant not to become irrelevant because of promise of secrecy or deception or because made when accused was drunk or in answer to questions.
When more persons than one are being tried jointly, a confession made by one of such persons affecting himself and some other of such persons is relevant.
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them.

Statements made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense, are relevant in certain cases.
Evidence given by a witness in a previous judicial proceeding is relevant in a subsequent proceeding if the witness cannot be found or is dead, etc.
Entries in books of account regularly kept in the course of business are relevant.
An entry in any public or official book, register or record, stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant, is relevant.
Statements in maps, charts and plans made by persons interested in them, are relevant.
Statements as to facts of public nature contained in certain acts or notifications are relevant.
Statements in any published book or journal relevant as forming part of literature or science are relevant.

When any statement of which evidence is given forms part of a longer statement or conversation, evidence shall be given of so much of the statement as is necessary to fully understand the nature of the statement.
Judgments of courts of justice, when relevant.
A final judgment, order or decree of a competent court is conclusive proof of legal character conferred by such judgment.
Judgments, orders or decrees other than those mentioned in Article 41 are relevant if they relate to matters of a public nature.
Judgments, etc., other than those covered under Article 41 and 42 are irrelevant unless the existence of such judgment is itself a fact in issue or is relevant under some other provision.
Any party to a suit may show that a judgment, order or decree which is relevant under Article 41 or 42 was delivered by a court not competent to deliver it.
Opinions of experts are relevant facts.
Facts bearing upon opinions of experts are relevant.
Opinion as to handwriting, when relevant.
Opinion as to existence of right or custom, when relevant.
Opinion as to usage, tenets, etc., when relevant.
Opinion on relationship, when relevant.
The burden of proof in a suit or proceeding lies on the person who would fail if no evidence were given on either side.
The burden of proof lies on the party who asserts the affirmative of the issue.
The burden of proof as to particular fact lies on person who wishes court to believe in its existence.
The burden of proving that a case falls within exceptions in law lies on the person asserting it.
The burden of proving fact especially within knowledge lies upon the person who has such knowledge.
When a fact is to be proved that supports presumption, the burden lies on the person opposing the presumption.
Court may presume the continuance of certain states of affairs.
Burden of proving that person is alive who has not been heard of for seven years lies on the person so asserting.
When a question arises whether a person is partner, landlord or agent, the burden lies on the person so asserting.
Possession is evidence of ownership; burden lies on the person denying ownership.
When a person has committed a crime, burden of proving self-defense lies on the accused.
The fact of legitimacy of a child born during marriage shall be presumed unless proven otherwise.
Court may presume certain facts from established facts unless evidence to the contrary is given.
Court may infer existence of any fact from other facts established in the case.
Estoppel: When a person has made another believe something true and act on it, he cannot deny its truth later.
Tenant or licensee cannot deny landlord's title at commencement of tenancy.
Accomplice is a competent witness and a conviction is not illegal merely because it proceeds on such testimony.
All persons are competent to testify unless the court considers them unable due to tender years, extreme old age, disease, or other cause.
Judge and Magistrate are not compelled to testify regarding their conduct in court except by special order of higher court.
Communications during marriage are privileged unless consent is given or a suit between spouses arises.
Evidence not permitted regarding affairs of state if it prejudices public interest.
Official communications are privileged unless head of department permits disclosure.
No public officer shall be compelled to disclose source of information as to commission of offence.
Professional communications between lawyer and client are privileged.
No advocate shall disclose client communication unless permitted or ordered.
Interpreters and clerks of advocates are bound by same confidentiality as advocates.
No one shall be compelled to disclose confidential communication with legal advisers.
The privilege may be waived by express consent of the client.
No person shall be compelled to produce unpublished official records unless ordered by superior authority.
Witnesses not excused from answering questions relevant to matter in issue, even if it may incriminate them.
No witness is compellable to answer any question if the answer will expose him to a penalty or forfeiture.
Court may compel witness to answer questions relevant to matter in issue.
Court may draw adverse inference against witness who refuses to answer.
No particular number of witnesses shall be required for proof of any fact.
Order in which evidence shall be produced is within discretion of the court.
Judge may ask any question in any form, at any time, and may order production of any document or thing.
Court shall decide case based on facts declared relevant under this Order and duly proved.
The provisions of this Order shall not derogate from any other law in force for the time being.
Repeal: The Evidence Act, 1872 is hereby repealed in Pakistan.
Short title, commencement and extent: This Order may be called the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984.
